summary: I was born in a refugee camp in Thailand after my family fled there following the communist takeover of Laos. Thailand was a temporary refuge for my Hmong people, who escaped political turmoil in our homeland. It was in Thailand that my family held onto crucial immigration documents, like our I-94 cards, which allowed us to enter the United States later. Though we lived in difficult conditions, Thailand was the place that marked the beginning of our journey towards a new life.

My family and I are originally from Laos, but we had to flee after the communist takeover there. The political changes in Laos forced many Hmong people, including my family, to seek safety elsewhere. We escaped into Thailand as refugees because staying in Laos was no longer safe for us. Laos is where our story began, but it was also the place we had to leave behind to survive.

In 1988, my family emigrated from Thailand to Minnesota, which became our new home in the United States. We arrived with very little, just our clothes and important immigration papers. Minnesota was where we started building our lives as political refugees, not economic immigrants. It was there that we applied for and received our green cards, and later, I took my citizenship oath in the U.S. It holds a special place in my journey towards becoming an American.

The United States became my family's new country when we entered in 1988 as political refugees from Thailand. It was here that I held my I-94 card, which proved my legal entry, and later, my green card, which allowed us to stay permanently. When I was nineteen, I applied for U.S. citizenship and took my oath in a ceremony with thousands of others, marking the end of my statelessness. Becoming a U.S. citizen was an emotional milestone, as it gave me a sense of belonging and identity after years of uncertainty.
